source_text: “I entreat you to observe that I have come here voluntarily, in response to that written appeal of a fellow-countryman which lies before you. I demand no more than the opportunity to do so without delay. Is not that my right?”

text: He set his cheeks upon his hands and so remained for a long while. His eyes looked before him without seeing. His thoughts drifted and widened and, little by little, grew dim. At last he drew his mind back to that final notion. “Only send the fiddle—without a word.”
source_text: He rested his cheeks between his hands, and remained so for a long time. His eyes stared unseeingly. His thoughts wandered and spread and faded. At length he recalled his mind to that last idea. "Just send the fiddle--without a word."

text: “I—I did not—I know full well I ought never to have entertained the notion of sending that valentine—forgive me, sir—it was a wanton act, such as no woman possessing the least self-respect ought to have committed. If you will but pardon my indiscretion, I swear I shall never again—”
source_text: “I—I didn’t—I know I ought never to have dreamt of sending that valentine—forgive me, sir—it was a wanton thing which no woman with any self-respect should have done. If you will only pardon my thoughtlessness, I promise never to—”

text: “No, no; not in the least, I assure you,” the other protested. “Your request does not fall precisely within the province of the reference department, but I shall be only too happy to assist you.”

text: “But there is yet time,” quoth Brissenden. “Who can tell but that you may yet prove the humble instrument of his salvation. Why did you not suffer me to swat him but once? I should have liked to have had a hand in it.”
source_text: “But there is yet time,” quoth Brissenden. “Who knows but what you may prove the humble instrument to save him. Why didn’t you let me swat him just once? I’d like to have had a hand in it.”

text: “They say, those vainglorious English, that it is the Refuge of many. Do you know of any compatriot who has found a Refuge there? A Doctor?”
source_text: “They say, those boastful English, that it is the Refuge of many. You know a compatriot who has found a Refuge there? A Doctor?”

text: “If you will go back again I shall—be something,” she said tenderly, laying her head close to his breast. “If you will consent, I will give you my promise, and not leave you waiting a single minute longer.”
source_text: “If you’ll go back again I’ll—be something,” she said tenderly, putting her head near his breast. “If you’ll agree I’ll give my promise, without making you wait a minute longer.”

text: “Those rooms are all in disorder; there has been hasty packing, and odds and ends lie upon the floor. There is no one in that room behind you! Let me look.”
source_text: “Those rooms are all in disorder, there has been hurried packing, there are odds and ends upon the ground. There is no one in that room behind you! Let me look.”

text: “I said as much! A brave number indeed! My fellow-citizen here would have it forty-two; ten additional heads are well worth the taking. The Guillotine works gallantly. I love it. Ho, forward! Whoop!”
source_text: “I said so! A brave number! My fellow-citizen here would have it forty-two; ten more heads are worth having. The Guillotine goes handsomely. I love it. Hi forward. Whoop!”

text: “Nephew, I believe—but at all events a degenerate successor—of the polished Marquis who was murdered,” said one. “I am happy to say I never knew him.”
source_text: “Nephew, I believe--but in any case degenerate successor--of the polished Marquis who was murdered,” said one. “Happy to say, I never knew him.”

text: “No, no, not at all,” Martin replied, with a shake of the hand and a motion toward the lone chair, while he himself seated himself upon the bed. “Yet how came you to know where I lived?”
source_text: “No, no, not at all,” Martin answered, shaking hands and waving him to the solitary chair, himself taking to the bed. “But how did you know where I lived?”

text: “If any one of these men, or all of these men, were inclined to spare him—which is a bold supposition, for what is his life, or any man’s, to them!—I question whether they would dare to spare him after the exhibition in the court.”
source_text: “If any one of these men, or all of these men, were disposed to spare him--which is a large supposition; for what is his life, or any man’s to them!--I doubt if they durst spare him after the demonstration in the court.”

text: “Go on!” cried the Dodger. “Why, where is your spirit? Have you no pride in yourself at all? Would you go and make yourself dependent upon your friends?”
source_text: “Go!” exclaimed the Dodger. “Why, where’s your spirit? Don’t you take any pride out of yourself? Would you go and be dependent on your friends?”

text: “You have guessed aright; I have of late been so deeply engaged in one pursuit that I have not allowed myself sufficient repose, as you perceive; but I trust, I most sincerely trust, that all these employments are now brought to a close, and that I am at last free.”
source_text: “You have guessed right; I have lately been so deeply engaged in one occupation that I have not allowed myself sufficient rest, as you see; but I hope, I sincerely hope, that all these employments are now at an end and that I am at length free.”

text: “A boy!” cried Mr. Giles, with great spirit, thrusting the tinker into the background. “What is the matter with the—eh?—Why, Brittles—look here—don’t you understand?”

text: “This manner of life of which you speak,” said she, “seems to me so vain and without purpose. Have you then no work in the world that might hold your interest more nearly?”
source_text: “This way of living that you speak of,” she said, “sounds so futile and purposeless. Haven’t you any work to do in the world that might interest you more?”

text: “Is that all, my dear Henry? How could you imagine that my first thought should not hasten to those dear, dear friends whom I love, and who are so worthy of my affection?”
source_text: “Is that all, my dear Henry? How could you suppose that my first thought would not fly towards those dear, dear friends whom I love and who are so deserving of my love?”

text: “Trouble not yourself as to what it is. Believe me in this: I cannot suffer myself to lose you. I must have you ever with me. Even this very evening I am loath to let you go. There is but one cure for this uneasiness, my dearest—you must be my wife.”
source_text: “Never mind what it is. Believe this, I cannot let myself lose you. I must have you always with me. This very evening I do not like to let you go. There is only one cure for this anxiety, dearest—you must be my wife.”

text: “Then I shall answer.” She braced herself to say it. “I do not think you were meant to write. Forgive me, dear. You force me to say it; and you know I know more of literature than you do.”
source_text: “Then I will answer.” She steeled herself to do it. “I don’t think you were made to write. Forgive me, dear. You compel me to say it; and you know I know more about literature than you do.”

source_text: The dread tribunal of five Judges, Public Prosecutor, and determined Jury, sat every day. text: “I was not in attendance at the ceremony; yet, in my judgment, you were,” said Carton. At this he laughed again, and they both laughed.

text:
As, winding hence, the throngs now part and disperse—but we, old man,  
Not for naught have I brought thee hither—we must remain,  
Thou to speak in thy turn, and I to listen and tell.
source_text:
As wending the crowds now part and disperse--but we old man,
Not for nothing have I brought you hither--we must remain,

text:
Who telleth a story of voiceless death?
Who raiseth the veil of the thing to come?
Who draweth the shades that are under the breadth
Of the winding caverns of the thronged tomb?
Or bindeth the hopes of what yet shall be
With the fears and the love for the things we see?
source_text:
Who telleth a tale of unspeaking death?
Who lifteth the veil of what is to come?
Who painteth the shadows that are beneath
The wide-winding caves of the peopled tomb?
Or uniteth the hopes of what shall be
With the fears and the love for that which we see?

text:
ORSINO:
There is no need of that. Attend: thou know’st
Olimpio, the castellan of Petrella
In old Colonna’s day; the man your father
Cast down from his office? And Marzio,
That desperate wretch, whom he but last year
Denied the meed of blood, well earned and due?
source_text:
ORSINO:
There is no need of that. Listen: you know
Olimpio, the castellan of Petrella
In old Colonna’s time; him whom your father
Degraded from his post? And Marzio,
That desperate wretch, whom he deprived last year
Of a reward of blood, well earned and due?

text:
Soft floated the music down the gentle air,
While fluent Greek, in vowelled undertone,
Was kept among the guests, discoursing low
At first, for scarcely yet had wine begun;
But when the fortunate vintage touch’d their brains,
The louder grew their speech, and louder rose the strains
Of potent instruments:--the gorgeous dyes,
The breadth, the splendour, and the draperies’ guise,
The roof of awful richness, nectarous cheer,
Fair slaves, and Lamia’s self, stood all forth near,
Now, when the wine hath done its rosy deed,
And every soul from human fetters freed,
No longer seem so strange; for merry wine, sweet wine,
Will make Elysian shades not over-fair, too divine.
Soon was God Bacchus at meridian height;
Flush’d were their cheeks, and bright eyes doubly bright:
Garlands of every green, and every scent
From vales despoil’d, or forest-branches rent,
In baskets of bright osier’d gold were brought
High as the handles heap’d, to suit the thought
Of every guest; that each, as he did please,
Might fancy-fit his brows, silk-pillow’d, at his ease.
source_text:
Soft went the music the soft air along,
While fluent Greek a vowel'd undersong
Kept up among the guests, discoursing low
At first, for scarcely was the wine at flow;
But when the happy vintage touch'd their brains,
Louder they talk, and louder come the strains
Of powerful instruments:--the gorgeous dyes,
The space, the splendour of the draperies,
The roof of awful richness, nectarous cheer,
Beautiful slaves, and Lamia's self, appear,
Now, when the wine has done its rosy deed,
And every soul from human trammels freed,
No more so strange; for merry wine, sweet wine,
Will make Elysian shades not too fair, too divine.
Soon was God Bacchus at meridian height;
Flush'd were their cheeks, and bright eyes double bright:
Garlands of every green, and every scent
From vales deflower'd, or forest-trees branch-rent,
In baskets of bright osier'd gold were brought
High as the handles heap'd, to suit the thought
Of every guest; that each, as he did please,
Might fancy-fit his brows, silk-pillow'd at his ease.

text:
They were the pride of the line of rangers,  
Peerless in horse, in rifle, song, and supper, in wooing,  
Stately, fierce, liberal, comely, haughty, and fond,  
Bearded, bronzed of sun, arrayed in the hunter’s free attire,  
Not one among them past thirty years of age.
source_text:
They were the glory of the race of rangers,
Matchless with horse, rifle, song, supper, courtship,
Large, turbulent, generous, handsome, proud, and affectionate,
Bearded, sunburnt, drest in the free costume of hunters,
Not a single one over thirty years of age.

text:
I sang of the wheeling stars,  
I sang of the crafty Earth,  
And of Heaven—and the Titan wars,  
And Love, and Death, and Birth,—  
And then I changed my piping,—  
Singing how down the vale of Maenalus  
I chased a maiden and embraced a reed.  
Gods and men, we are all beguiled thus!  
It bursts within our bosom and then we bleed:  
All wept, as I deem both ye now would,  
If envy or age had not frozen your blood,  
At the grief of my sweet piping.
source_text:
I sang of the dancing stars,
I sang of the daedal Earth,
And of Heaven—and the giant wars,
And Love, and Death, and Birth,—
And then I changed my pipings,—
Singing how down the vale of Maenalus
I pursued a maiden and clasped a reed.
Gods and men, we are all deluded thus!
It breaks in our bosom and then we bleed:
All wept, as I think both ye now would,
If envy or age had not frozen your blood,
At the sorrow of my sweet pipings.

text:
Grieve not thus, dear mother, (the just-grown daughter speaks through
her sobs,
The little sisters cluster close in silence, pale with dread,)
See, dearest mother, the letter saith Pete shall soon be better.
source_text:

her sobs,

See, dearest mother, the letter says Pete will soon be better.

text:
And is this death?—The pyre hath vanished clean,  
The Pestilence, the Tyrant, and the throng;  
The flames fall hush’d—by slow degrees is seen  
The music of a breath-detaining song,  
Which, like the kiss of love when life is young,  
Steeps the faint eyes in darkness sweet and deep;  
With ever-shifting notes it glides along,  
Till on my passive soul there seem’d to creep  
A melody, like waves on wrinkled sands that leap.
source_text:
And is this death?—The pyre has disappeared,
The Pestilence, the Tyrant, and the throng;
The flames grow silent—slowly there is heard
The music of a breath-suspending song,
Which, like the kiss of love when life is young,
Steeps the faint eyes in darkness sweet and deep;
With ever-changing notes it floats along,
Till on my passive soul there seemed to creep
A melody, like waves on wrinkled sands that leap.

text:

The birds more jocund sang;
The sunshine flung his hat aside,
The orchard’s spangles hung.

text:
I sat me by my warpin-wheel,
And aye I drave it round;
But every shot, and every knock,
My heart it gave a wound.
To the weaver’s, &c.
source_text:

text:
By all I lov’d, forsaken and forgot,
No friendly visage ever lights my squalid cot;
Shunn’d, hated, wrong’d, unpitied, unredrest,
The scornful jest that mock’d me to the rest!
Ev’n the poor stay of my wretched life,
Torn from me by the violence of legal strife.
Oft grateful for my very daily bread
To those on whom my family’s large bounty fed;
A welcome inmate at their homely fare,
My griefs, my woes, my sighs, my tears they share:
(Their vulgar souls unlike the souls refin’d,
The fashioned marble of the polish’d mind).
source_text:
By all I lov’d, neglected and forgot,
No friendly face e’er lights my squalid cot;
Shunn’d, hated, wrong’d, unpitied, unredrest,
The mock’d quotation of the scorner’s jest!
Ev’n the poor support of my wretched life,
Snatched by the violence of legal strife.
Oft grateful for my very daily bread
To those my family’s once large bounty fed;
A welcome inmate at their homely fare,
My griefs, my woes, my sighs, my tears they share:
(Their vulgar souls unlike the souls refin’d,
The fashioned marble of the polished mind).

but words be wind.”
source_text: “And I thought so too, Mrs. Yeobright,” said Christian earnestly, “but Father there was so eager that he had no manners at all, and left home almost afore ’twas dark. I told him ’twas barely decent in a’ old man to come so oversoon; but words be wind.”

text:

The pride o’ the place and the country around a’;
Their carriage and dress, a stranger would guess,
In Lon’on or Paris, they’d gotten it a’.

text:
Why joys are meted out so thin,
Why Paradise delays,
Why floods are portioned into bowls, --
I ponder not these ways.
source_text:

Why Paradise defer,
Why floods are served to us in bowls, --
I speculate no more.

text:
PROMETHEUS:
It doth afflict me: speech is swift and vain;
For a brief while grief is blind, and so was mine.
I would no living creature suffer pain.
source_text:
PROMETHEUS:
It doth repent me: words are quick and vain;
Grief for awhile is blind, and so was mine.
I wish no living thing to suffer pain.

text:
Is there a bard of homely song,
Who, unregarded, moves the throng,
That weekly to this place belong,
O, pass him by?
Nay, with a brother-feeling strong,
Here, breathe a sigh.
source_text:

O, pass not by!

text:

Squire Pope but trims his skinkle-patches
O’ heathen tatters:
I pass by hunders, nameless wretches,
That ape their betters.

text:
And oftentimes unto those streams of upper air  
That whirl the earth through all its daily round,  
She would ascend, and win the spirits there  
To let her join their chorus. Mortals found  
That on those days the sky was calm and fair,  
And mystic snatches of harmonious sound  
Wandered upon the earth where’er she passed,  
And happy thoughts of hope, too sweet to last.

text:

Honor to the ancient bow-string!  
Honor to the bugle-horn!  
Honor to the forests unshorn!  
Honor to the Lincoln green!  
Honor to the archer keen!  
Honor to little, tight John,  
And the steed he rode upon!  
Honor to bold Robin Hood,  
Slumbering in the underwood!  
Honor to Maid Marian,  
And to all the Sherwood clan!  
Though their days have sped away

source_text:
So it is: yet let us sing,
Honour to the old bow-string!
Honour to the bugle-horn!
Honour to the woods unshorn!
Honour to the Lincoln green!
Honour to the archer keen!
Honour to tight little John,
And the horse he rode upon!
Honour to bold Robin Hood,
Sleeping in the underwood!
Honour to maid Marian,
And to all the Sherwood-clan!
Though their days have hurried by
Let us two a burden try.

text:
Follow unto the deep wood’s weeds,
Follow unto the wild-briar dingle,
Where we seek to blend and mingle,
And the violet breathes her tale
To the odour-laden gale,
For those twain have enough to do
Of such task as I and you.
source_text:

text:
MEPHISTOPHELES:
I feel no such enchantment stirring here.
My frame is all of winter, and I fain
Would have the flowers along our road be frost and snow.
But lo, how melancholy now ascends,
Half-lifting faintly her delayed pale beam,
The blank and unwelcome circle of the red moon,
And lends so evil a light, that every pace
Doth trip against some crag. With your leave,
I’ll summon Ignis-fatuus to our help:
I spy one there ablaze so merrily.
Halloo, my friend! I pray you, may I ask
That you would grace us with your shining company?
Why should you burn away there all in vain?
Pray be so kind as guide us with your light.
source_text:
MEPHISTOPHELES:
Nothing of such an influence do I feel.
My body is all wintry, and I wish
The flowers upon our path were frost and snow.
But see how melancholy rises now,
Dimly uplifting her belated beam,
The blank unwelcome round of the red moon,
And gives so bad a light, that every step
One stumbles ’gainst some crag. With your permission,
I’ll call on Ignis-fatuus to our aid:
I see one yonder burning jollily.
Halloo, my friend! may I request that you
Would favour us with your bright company?
Why should you blaze away there to no purpose?
Pray be so good as light us up this way.

text:
Wave, wave high the banner!  
When Freedom rides forth to conquest by:  
Though the slaves that fan her  
Be Famine and Toil, breathing sigh for sigh.  
And ye who attend her imperial car,  
Lift not your hands in the banded war,  
But in her defence, whose children ye are.

text:
I’m glad I cannot credit it,
For it would still my breath,
And I should like to look a little more
Upon so curious an earth!
I’m glad that they did credit it
Whom I have never found
Since that great autumn afternoon
I left them in the ground.
source_text:
I 'm glad I don't believe it,
For it would stop my breath,
And I 'd like to look a little more
At such a curious earth!
I am glad they did believe it
Whom I have never found
Since the mighty autumn afternoon
I left them in the ground.

text:
Farewell to the mountains, with snow on their head,  
Farewell to the straths and green valleys below spread;  
Farewell to the forests and wild-drooping woods,  
Farewell to the torrents and loud-rushing floods.  
My heart’s in the Highlands, &c.
source_text:
Farewell to the mountains, high-cover’d with snow,
Farewell to the straths and green vallies below;
Farewell to the forests and wild-hanging woods,
Farewell to the torrents and loud-pouring floods.
My heart’s in the Highlands, &c.

text:
The passage to and fro the magazine is now barred by the sentinels,  
So many strange visages they spy, they know not whom to trust.
source_text:
The transit to and from the magazine is now stopt by the sentinels,
They see so many strange faces they do not know whom to trust.

text:
There is no halting, nor can there ever be halting,  
If I, thou, and the worlds, and all beneath or above their surfaces,  
were this very moment brought back to a pallid drift, it would  
not profit the long course,  
We should assuredly rise again where now we stand,  
And surely go as much farther, and then farther and farther.
source_text:
There is no stoppage and never can be stoppage,
If I, you, and the worlds, and all beneath or upon their surfaces,
were this moment reduced back to a pallid float, it would
not avail the long run,
We should surely bring up again where we now stand,
And surely go as much farther, and then farther and farther.

text:
Banner and Pennant:  
Yet loftier, louder, mightier, bard! yet farther, broader cleave!  
No more let our children count us but for riches and for peace alone,  
We may be terror and slaughter, and are so now,  
Not now are we any one of these broad and haughty States, (nor  
any five, nor ten,)  
Nor mart nor depot are we, nor money-bank in the city,  
But these and all, and the brown and widening land, and the mines  
beneath, are ours,  
And the shores of the sea are ours, and the rivers great and small,  
And the fields they water, and the crops and the fruits are ours,  
Bays and channels and ships sailing in and out are ours—while we above all,  
Above the expanse spread underneath, the three or four millions of square  
miles, the capitals,  
The forty millions of people,—O bard! in life and death supreme,  
We, even we, henceforth fling forth masterful, high up above,  
Not for the present only, for a thousand years chanting through thee,  
This song to the soul of one poor little child.
source_text:
Banner and Pennant:
Yet louder, higher, stronger, bard! yet farther, wider cleave!
No longer let our children deem us riches and peace alone,
We may be terror and carnage, and are so now,
Not now are we any one of these spacious and haughty States, (nor
any five, nor ten,)
Nor market nor depot we, nor money-bank in the city,
But these and all, and the brown and spreading land, and the mines
below, are ours,
And the shores of the sea are ours, and the rivers great and small,
And the fields they moisten, and the crops and the fruits are ours,
Bays and channels and ships sailing in and out are ours--while we over all,
Over the area spread below, the three or four millions of square
miles, the capitals,
The forty millions of people,--O bard! in life and death supreme,
We, even we, henceforth flaunt out masterful, high up above,
Not for the present alone, for a thousand years chanting through you,
This song to the soul of one poor little child.

text:
Robin vow’d to me  
A’ my winter fare;  
Fient haet he had but three  
Guse-feathers and a whittle!  
Robin shure, &c.
source_text:
Robin promis’d me
A’ my winter vittle;
Fient haet he had but three
Guse-feathers and a whittle!
Robin shure, &c.

text:
The cold earth lay asleep below,  
And over it the cold sky glowed;  
And all around, with a shivering sound,  
From ice-walled caves and snowy fields,  
Night’s breath, like death, was drifting forth  
Beneath the sinking moon.
source_text:
The cold earth slept below,
Above the cold sky shone;
And all around, with a chilling sound,
From caves of ice and fields of snow,
The breath of night like death did flow
Beneath the sinking moon.

text:
We may dream, in that prolonged sleep,
That we are not those that weep;
E’en as Pleasure dreams of thee,
Life-abandoning Misery,
Thou mayst dream of her with me.
source_text:
We may dream, in that long sleep,
That we are not those who weep;
E’en as Pleasure dreams of thee,
Life-deserting Misery,
Thou mayst dream of her with me.

text:
"'T is daisy in the briefest;
A little farther on,
Nearest the door to rouse the first,
Tiny leontodon.
source_text:
"'T is daisy in the shortest;
A little farther on,
Nearest the door to wake the first,
Little leontodon.

text:
As they come unto an end,
Of that which lies beneath the earlier songs—of my intent in them,
Of the seed I have striven to sow in them,
Of joy, sweet joy, through many a year, in them,
(For them, for them have I lived, in them my task is done,)
Of many a dear aspiration, of many a dream and design;
Through Space and Time blended in a chant, and the flowing eternal sameness,
To Nature, enfolding these, enfolding God—to the joyous,
electric all,
To the thought of Death, and taking up exultant Death in its turn
as one with life,
The advent of man to sing;
To draw together you, ye parted, diverse lives,
To make accord of mountains and rocks and streams,
And the winds of the north, and the forests of oak and pine,
With you O soul.
source_text:
As they draw to a close,
Of what underlies the precedent songs--of my aims in them,
Of the seed I have sought to plant in them,
Of joy, sweet joy, through many a year, in them,
(For them, for them have I lived, in them my work is done,)
Of many an aspiration fond, of many a dream and plan;
Through Space and Time fused in a chant, and the flowing eternal identity,
To Nature encompassing these, encompassing God--to the joyous,
electric all,
To the sense of Death, and accepting exulting in Death in its turn
the same as life,
The entrance of man to sing;
To compact you, ye parted, diverse lives,
To put rapport the mountains and rocks and streams,
And the winds of the north, and the forests of oak and pine,
With you O soul.

text:
Out of the murk of heaviest clouds,
Out of the feudal wrecks and heap’d-up skeletons of kings,
Out of that ancient whole of European debris, the shatter’d mummeries,
Ruin’d cathedrals, crumble of palaces, tombs of priests,
Lo, Freedom’s features fresh and undimm’d look forth—the same immortal
face looks forth;
(A glimpse as of thy Mother’s face, Columbia,
A flash, significant as of a sword,
Beaming toward thee.)
source_text:
Out of the murk of heaviest clouds,
Out of the feudal wrecks and heap’d-up skeletons of kings,
Out of that old entire European debris, the shatter’d mummeries,
Ruin’d cathedrals, crumble of palaces, tombs of priests,
Lo, Freedom’s features fresh undimm’d look forth--the same immortal
face looks forth;
(A glimpse as of thy Mother’s face Columbia,
A flash significant as of a sword,
Beaming towards thee.)

text:
The sun, as usual, went abroad,  
The flowers, as they were wont, did blow,  
As though no soul had crossed the solstice  
That maketh all things new.
source_text:
The sun, as common, went abroad,
The flowers, accustomed, blew,
As if no soul the solstice passed
That maketh all things new.

text:
So steadfast in its stolid trust,
The shaft that never knew,
It shames the constancy that fled
Ere its own emblem flew.
source_text:
So constant to its stolid trust,
The shaft that never knew,
It shames the constancy that fled
Before its emblem flew.

text:
The crimson on the hill
Doth steal away my will;
If any mock or jeer,
Take heed, for God is here,
That is all.
source_text:
The red upon the hill
Taketh away my will;
If anybody sneer,
Take care, for God is here,
That's all.

text:
She talk’d, she smil’d, my heart she beguil’d;
She charm’d my soul, I wist not how;
And aye the stound, the deadly wound,
Came frae her een sae bonie blue.
Yet “spare to speak, and spare to speed;”
She’ll aiblins hear unto my vow:
Should she refuse, I’ll lay my dead
To her twa een sae bonie blue.
source_text:
She talk’d, she smil’d, my heart she wyl’d;
She charm’d my soul I wist na how;
And aye the stound, the deadly wound,
Cam frae her een so bonie blue.
But “spare to speak, and spare to speed;”
She’ll aiblins listen to my vow:
Should she refuse, I’ll lay my dead
To her twa een sae bonie blue.

text:
Wild beats my heart to trace thy steps,
Whose fathers, in days of yore,
Through hostile ranks and ruin’d gaps
Old Scotia’s bloody lion bore:
Ev’n I, who sing in rustic lore,
Haply my sires have left their shed,
And fac’d grim Danger’s loudest roar,
Bold-following where thy fathers led!
source_text:
Wild beats my heart to trace your steps,
Whose ancestors, in days of yore,
Thro’ hostile ranks and ruin’d gaps
Old Scotia’s bloody lion bore:
Ev’n I who sing in rustic lore,
Haply my sires have left their shed,
And fac’d grim Danger’s loudest roar,
Bold-following where your fathers led!

text:
There’s naught but care on every hand,
In every hour that glides, O:
What means the life of mortal man,
An’ wert it not for the lasses, O.
Green grow, &c.
source_text:
There’s nought but care on ev’ry han’,
In ev’ry hour that passes, O:
What signifies the life o’ man,
An’ ’twere na for the lasses, O.
Green grow, &c.
